title: Unemployment and marriage status
gist: FRED data show married men's and married women's unemployment rates moved roughly in sync but were about 2 points apart until converging in the 1990s, while women who head households (single parents or main breadwinners) have markedly higher unemployment because marital status changes labor-force search incentives, not just job availability.

themes.theme: What the unemployment rate actually measures

insights.claim: The unemployment rate is not the share of the general population that isn't working; it is the fraction of the labor force (those working plus those actively looking) who are not working but are actively available and searching for a job — so voluntary labor-force exit doesn't automatically show up as unemployment.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: When reading headline unemployment prints, check labor-force participation alongside them — a falling unemployment rate can reflect people exiting the labor force rather than genuine job gains.
insights.quote: The unemployment rate is the fraction of persons in the total labor force who are not working but are actively available and looking for a job.
insights.locator: To be clear: The unemployment rate is not the fraction

themes.theme: Marriage and the gender unemployment gap

insights.claim: In FRED's Current Population Survey series, the unemployment rate for married men and married women moved in sync across business cycles but were separated by about 2 percentage points in the early years of the data before converging during the 1990s.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: The 1990s convergence marks a structural shift in married women's labor-market attachment worth noting when comparing pre- vs post-1990s gender labor data.
insights.quote: In the early years, these two series were separated by about 2 percentage points until they converged in the 1990s.

insights.claim: Women who maintain families — meaning single-parent households or households where the woman is the main breadwinner — have a markedly and consistently higher unemployment rate than married women.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Household-headship status is a sharper predictor of labor-market vulnerability than gender alone; policy or labor-market stress analysis should segment by household role, not just sex.
insights.quote: The unemployment rate is considerably higher for women who maintain families—in single-parent households or in households where they’re the main breadwinner.
insights.locator: There are a few stories revealed in this graph

insights.claim: When one spouse in a married couple already has a job, the other spouse faces lower incentive to work than a single person would, and may voluntarily stop working and exit the labor force entirely rather than searching.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Dual-earner household formation can mechanically shrink measured labor supply as one partner opts out, a dynamic to watch in labor-force participation trends, not just payroll counts.
insights.quote: If one spouse in a married couple has a job, the incentives for the other spouse to work can be lower than a single person’s incentives.

insights.claim: A non-working spouse who is opportunistic about finding work may only register as being in the labor force once they actually land a job, which would tend to lower the measured unemployment rate for that group.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Opportunistic (rather than continuous) job search among secondary earners can understate labor-market slack in official statistics.
insights.quote: If the non-working spouse is opportunistic about finding a job, they may be included in the labor force only when they have a job. This would lower the unemployment rate.

insights.claim: Women who maintain families need a job more urgently than married women, so they search more intensely and frequently and are less likely to opportunistically abandon their job search, which is the stated reason their measured unemployment rate runs markedly higher.
insights.speaker: St. Louis Fed — FRED Blog
insights.so_what: Higher unemployment among household heads reflects more persistent job search effort, not necessarily worse job-finding odds — relevant when interpreting this subgroup's unemployment rate as a hardship indicator.
insights.quote: They may need a job much more than married women. Hence, they may look much more intensely and frequently for a job and are less likely to be opportunistic about it and abandon their job search.
